item.title: Not really liking this
item.content:encoded:
While i did enjoy the descriptions of the battle the rest was kind of underwhelming and there is not really a story to make up for it

Also we already have a gashadokuro skip in SCP-2863

-1 from me

item.title: Clinical-ism error
item.content:encoded: I would recommend changing parts that refer to the victim of the ritual from 'they' or 'them' to 'it' or 'the subject'. For example, "placing it in their mouth and forcing them to swallow." should be changed to 'placing it in the subject's mouth and forcing it to swallow." The Foundation is supposed to have a clinical tone even when it is describing a victim that is not yet anomalous. I have not read a single article besides this on the site (in recent memory) that uses 'they' since, like I said, skips should be clinical. That being said this was overall an enjoyable read as I do not see many takes on modern mythology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree. You don't need the Ethics Committee to tell you that torturing someone for three weeks until they turn into a vore monster is wrong. Putting it plainly like that shows the humanity of the researchers, and telegraphs the horror of what's coming next.

SCP-4465 does the same thing, for similar reasons.

item.title: Re: An Error
item.content:encoded: The people that originally spoke Moldavian, a culture called the Csangos, have their origins in Hungary before they migrated to Romanian in more modern times. This distinction is deliberate on my part. He is *not* a modern Romanian.

item.title: An Error
item.content:encoded: According to Wikipedia, Moldavian is a dialect of Romanian, not Hungarian.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hello! I love the concept and the execution! My only nitpick is that "Due to ethical concerns, this cannot be verified." (at the end of the description) it doesn't read as clinical to me. I feel a mention of the Ethics Committee in some capacity, would make it read more as clinical.

Nice article!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Should be fixed now. I left immediately after posting but Malyce was nice enough to amend for me (with my okay).

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
Credit where credit is due to Shigeaki Yamauchi's "World's Bizarre Thriller Complete Works 2: Monsters of the World" (1972) which is where the yokai known as Gashadokuro first appeared. Being a ravenous fan of mythology, I was happy to have consumed this idea for nightmare fuel and regurgitated it into this, even if it's a "modern creation" versus other myth and legend.

Thank you to MalyceGraves for their critical feedback and grammar hammer
